shower.thought: We spend our lives trying to be memorable, but the people we'll remember most are usually the ones who made us feel like we were the only person in the room.
Your phone knows you better than you know yourself, which means a stranger with your password might understand your priorities more accurately than you do.
The worst part about growing up isn't losing your imagination—it's developing the ability to imagine all the ways things can go wrong.
You can't actually remember most of your life, which means you're mostly living in a story you tell yourself about who you are.
People apologize for being "too much," but nobody apologizes for being too little—we just call it professionalism.
The moment you accept that you might be wrong about something is the moment you stop learning anything new about it.
We're nostalgic for periods of our lives where we were actively miserable because at least the misery made sense.
If you had to explain why you love someone to a stranger, you'd probably start to wonder if the reasons are enough.
Most arguments aren't about being right—they're about not wanting to be the kind of person who was wrong.
You only realize what you actually wanted after you've gotten something else instead.
The people who seem most confident are often just the ones who've decided to stop apologizing for their choices internally.
Time moves slower when you're waiting for something and faster when you're running from it, which might explain why anxiety exists.
Your eight-year-old self would be disappointed by what you've decided to care about—or relieved.
We call it "networking" when we're trying to use people and "friendship" when it works out anyway.
You can't unhear a song that gets stuck in your head, which is basically how trauma works but with memories instead.
The internet has given us access to infinite knowledge and somehow made us less sure about anything.
Regret is the only emotion that can feel worse the longer you think about it—everything else eventually dulls.
Most people don't change their minds; they just find new people who already agree with them.
You're nostalgic for conversations you had that you can't fully remember, which means you miss how the conversation made you feel, not the conversation itself.
We're all just one mental breakdown away from radically reorganizing our priorities and calling it personal growth.

motivational.thought: The only way to do great work is to love what you are doing. — Steve Jobs
Your limitation—it's only your imagination. Push beyond it. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of joy, but progress is the cure for despair. — Unknown
You are not behind. You are not lost. You are exactly where you need to be, becoming who you're meant to become. — Unknown
The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
Don't wait for the perfect moment. Take the moment and make it perfect. — Unknown
The strongest version of you isn't found in comfort—it's built in the struggle. — Unknown
You will face criticism no matter what you do. Do it anyway. — Unknown
Small daily progress is still progress. Slow is fast enough when you're moving forward. — Unknown
Your past does not determine your future. Your next decision does. — Unknown
The price of discipline is far less than the price of regret. — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems. — James Clear
Doubt kills more dreams than failure ever will. — Unknown
Everything worth having costs something. The question is: what are you willing to pay? — Unknown
Invest in yourself. No one else will care as much about your growth as you do. — Unknown
The discomfort you feel right now is the exact place where growth happens. — Unknown
Perfection is a thief that steals progress. Done is better than perfect. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you make this decision. Make them proud. — Unknown
You don't need permission to start. You just need the courage to begin. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my therapist I feel like nobody listens to me.
He said, "That's great, keep going."

My parents said they'd always be there for me.
They weren't wrong — they just had no other options.
I have a stepladder because my real ladder left when I was just a kid.

My girlfriend asked if I'd die for her.
I said yes, but we should probably try some other stuff first.
I've been on so many diets, my body has trust issues now.

Whenever someone says "no offense," I know they're about to say something offensive.
I respect the warning system — like a rattlesnake, but with opinions.

My motivational poster says "You miss 100% of the shots you don't take."
Tell that to my dad.

I asked my boss if I'd ever make partner at the firm.
He said, "Let's talk about realistic goals."
We didn't talk about much after I stopped coming in.
My life is like a superhero origin story, except I'm the collateral damage.

I told my friend he was being paranoid about his girlfriend cheating.
Turns out he wasn't.
Now I'm the bad guy for the facts.

The worst thing about having social anxiety is the irony of wanting to leave the party but being too afraid to say goodbye.
So you just hide in the bathroom until everyone forgets you exist.

My doctor said I should get more exercise.
I told him I do — I run from my problems every day.
He said that doesn't count.
I'm looking for a new doctor.

My parents always said I could be anything I wanted.
They didn't specify it had to be successful.

I tried to make a joke about my depression.
My therapist said that's actually a healthy coping mechanism.
My friends said it was just sad.
Turns out they were both right.

I love when people ask "how are you?" and actually wait for an answer.
It's a nice way to filter out my real friends from the ones who regret asking.

My dating app profile says "must love dogs."
Mostly because I'm more comfortable around them than people.
Also, I have three at home, so it narrows things down.

I said goodbye to my childhood trauma yesterday.
It said, "See you tonight in your dreams."
We both knew it was telling the truth.

My mother-in-law asked if I was good enough for her daughter.
I said, "Probably not, but here we are."
She appreciated the honesty more than my wife did.

I used to want to be a doctor.
Then I realized I just liked having power over people.
Now I'm a manager.

People say money can't buy happiness.
But it can buy medication, which is close enough.
